# Transcrição: 07-Eating my Rabbit

- **Fonte:** 07-Eating my Rabbit
- **Duração:** 07:29
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:00]

I'm sitting at the dining room table in my girlfriend's parents' home. I'm eating a cookie. My girlfriend, Lisa, is sitting next to me and she's talking, but I don't hear a word she says. I'm staring at the front door. I'm waiting for it to open. I'm waiting for her father to come home. I'm a 19-year-old boy about to meet his girlfriend's father for the first time and I am terrified. When he walks in, I stand up and we meet in the middle and we shake hands and as he puts his hand over mine, I can feel the calluses on his hands and the roughness of the skin. I can see the grease under his fingers and I know this is not going to go well for me. These are man's hands. These are the hands of someone who can fix plumbing and slide under a car and change the oil. This is a man who can take down a tree and then put it back up again if he needs to. This is a guy who builds and repairs things and I don't have those hands. My hands are soft. Mine are hands that play Miss Pac-Man on Friday nights and Framingham at the arcade. I have hands that roll 20-sided dice on card tables and basements while playing Dungeons and Dragons. My hands play the flute. And when people aren't looking, I have hands that hold shell silver steam poetry and read it silently. These are not hands that build and repair. They are hands that purchase and replace. And so I know this isn't going to go well for me because it didn't go well for me before. Before Lisa, there was Laura, my high school girlfriend. Laura's father was named Butch and he was the dictionary definition of that word. Shaking Butch's hands was like sticking your hand in a bag of broken glass. I met Butch in his driveway next to a stump grinder, a bucket truck and a bulldozer. And when I asked him, what do you do for a living Butch, he said, whatever needs to be done. And I knew I was screwed because I am not a man who can do whatever needs to be done. I pay people to do what needs to be done. But I want this to work with Lisa's father. I really do. So I want to make a plan, but I know I'm already in trouble. Lisa's father is a car guy. I used to drive a 1976 Chevy Malibu with a 350 V8. I don't actually know what any of those numbers mean, but I know guys love those numbers. But now I drive a Toyota Tercel powder blue. He sees my car. I swear he wants to punch me in the face. He asked me where I live. I tell him Attenborough, but I can never take him to my house. It is a boy's house. It's got Bart Simpson posters all over the walls. We have two rabbits that we got because we thought girls would think they were cute and hang around longer, which actually works.

## [03:00 - 06:02]

But we've fallen in love with these rabbits, but they hop around our house like cats. And we have 25 hamsters and habit trails all over the house with like tubes that connect all the habit trails. It is steampunk hamster world. Wherever you stand, there's a hamster over your head. I cannot bring this man into this world of boys, but I'm going to try. I'm going to make a plan. And the first plan is I'm going to get him to like me. He's Portuguese right off the boat. So I decided I'm going to learn about Portugal, which in 1991 is a thing because to learn anything in 91, you have to go to a government building during office hours and find a card catalog and pull out a card to find the book. And none of those books are equipped with control F. So you have to read the whole fucking thing to find the three things that you want to know about Portugal. And I do. I learned about the age of exploration. Portugal kicked ass in the age of exploration. And I tell him I knew it. And then I find this hutch of rabbits in his backyard. He loves rabbits too. And at this point, our rabbits are chewing through the cords. We've lost our TV. We're down a lamp. We're going to be in the dark before you know it. So I love my rabbits, but I offer them to this man and he takes them. And as he does, I swear we take a step closer together. And then little things happen. Like instead of calling me, hey, you, he calls me Matt. And instead of growling, he grunts. And it never becomes a full smile, but it stops being a scowl. And then the holidays come around and he knows I'm alone. And so he invites me to Thanksgiving dinner, which is a big deal for me because the last three thanksgivings for me have been Domino's pizza and the Dallas Cowboys and despair. And so to be able to go to a house with an actual Thanksgiving is fantastic. And I go and his whole family is there. There's 25 people around the table and he's saved a spot right next to himself for me with Lisa on the other side. And for the first time in a long time, I have family people that I believe want me there. And there is Turkey and there's ham and there's football and I am loving every minute of it. And he is almost managing a smile when he asks me, what do you think of that stew? And I say it's fantastic. And he says, you should like it. It's your rabbit. And I can't believe it. I will find out later that the Portuguese love rabbit. It was not in the fucking book. And I will find out that he sells rabbits to restaurants, which is why he has so many. But in this moment, he thinks this is funny. So I turn to Lisa to see if she's in on it and she's just as appalled as I am. And so I turn back to him and he is smiling at me for the first time. And it is probably three seconds that I stare at him, but it feels like three minutes with a rage builds. And then a boy stands up. And by the time I get to my feet, I am a man for the first time in my life. And I let this man know exactly how I feel.

## [06:02 - 07:29]

I let him have it at that table. I swear at him in front of 25 of his family members. And then I walk out the door. My hands are still soft today. I cannot fix a thing. My wife fixes more things in our house. Actually my wife fixes everything in our house. And I still read Shell Silverstein. And I still desperately want to play the flute. But I have learned that my soft hands have nothing to do with being a man. That my hands can be as soft as I want them to be. Because a man is a person who can stand up and say what he believes and what is in his heart and what needs to be said in the moment it needs to be said. That is the day truly that I became a man.
